title: Smt Girijamma W/O Sri Parameshwarappa ... vs Smt Mallamma W/O Halappa D/O Muddappa on 28 April, 2026
body: Smt Girijamma W/O Sri Parameshwarappa ... vs Smt Mallamma W/O Halappa D/O Muddappa on 28 April, 2026 -1- NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR IN THE HIGH COURT OF KARNATAKA, AT DHARWAD DATED THIS THE 28TH DAY OF APRIL 2026 BEFORE THE HON'BLE MRS JUSTICE GEETHA K.B. REGULAR SECOND APPEAL NO. 100907 OF 2025 (FDP) BETWEEN: 1. SMT. GIRIJAMMA W/O. SRI. PARAMESHWARAPPA, D/O. LATE CHIKKAPLA CHANNABASAPPA, AGED ABOUT 70 YEARS, OCC: NIL, R/O. ALAGANCHIKERE VILLAGE, YEDEHALLI POST, TQ. HARAPANAHALLI, DIST. VIJAYANAGARA. 2. SMT. BASAMMA W/O. LATE K. PARAMESHWARAPPA D/O. LATE CHIKKAPLA CHANNABASAPPA, AGED ABOUT 65 YEARS, OCC: NIL, 3. SRI. VENKATESH, AGE: 30 YEARS, R/O: AVARAGOLLA VILLAGE, TQ & DIST: DAVANAGERE- 577001. -8- NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR 27. SRI. SWAMY VIJAY S/O. SURYAVATHI, AGE: 28 YEARS, R/O: AVARAGOLLA, TQ & DIST: DAVANAGERE- 577001. 28. SRI. IJARI CHANNAPPA S/O. LATE YAMUNAPPA, AGE: 60 YEARS, OCC: AGRICULTURIST, R/O: CHIKKAMEGALAGERE, TQ: HARAPANAHALLI, DIST: BALLARI- 583101. 29. SMT. NAGARATHNAMMA W/O. LATE. G.B. MRUTHYUNJAYAPPA, AGED MAJOR, OCC: NIL, 30. SMT. KAVITHA D/O. LATE G.B. MRUTHYUNJAYAPPA, AGED: MAJOR, OCC: NIL, 31. SMT. SAVITHA D/O. LATE G.B. MRUTHYUNJAYAPPA, AGED@ MAJOR, OCC: NIL, 32. SMT. PAVITHRA D/O. LATE G.B. MRUTHYUNJAYAPPA, RESPONDENT NO. 29 TO 32 ARE R/O: 285/1 MAHARAJA PETE, NEAR CHOWKI PETE, DAVANAGERE- 583101. ...RESPONDENTS (BY SMT. SURABHI KULKARNI, ADVOCATE FOR R1-R6; R7 TO R32- NOTICE DISPENSED WITH) THIS REGULAR SECOND APPEAL IS FILED UNDER SECTION 100 OF CPC PRAYING TO SET ASIDE THE JUDGMENT AND DECREE DATED 28.10.2025 MADE IN RA NO.2/2022 PASSED BY THE SENIOR CIVIL JUDGE AND JMFC HARAPANAHALLI THEREBY CONFIRMING THE JUDGMENT AND FINAL DECREE DATED 15-12- 2021 MADE IN FDP NO.2/2007 PASSED BY THE CIVIL JUDGE AND JMFC HARAPANAHALLI AND ETC. -9- NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR THIS APPEAL COMING ON FOR FINAL HEARING THIS DAY, JUDGMENT WAS DELIVERED THEREIN AS UNDER: CORAM: THE HON'BLE MRS JUSTICE GEETHA K.B. ORAL JUDGMENT 1. This is the appeal filed under Section 100 CPC, praying for setting aside the judgment and decree dated 28.10.2025 passed in R.A.No.02/2022 on the file of Senior Civil Judge and JMFC, Harapanahalli (hereinafter referred to as 'the First Appellate Court', for short), confirming the order drawing up of final decree dated 15.12.2021 passed in FDP No.2/2007 on the file of the Civil Judge and JMFC, Harapanahalli (hereinafter referred to as 'the FDP Court', for short). 2. The parties would be referred with their ranks, as they were before Trial Court for sake of convenience and clarity. 3. The petitioners have filed FDP No.2/2007 praying for drawing up of final decree in terms of preliminary decree passed in respect of petition 'A' and 'B' - 10 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR schedule properties; for appointment of Court Commissioner under Section 54 CPC for measurement of boundaries of 1/3rd share of original plaintiff in Schedule 'B' properties and to handover possession to petitioners/applicants. 4. It is stated in the FDP petition that O.S.No.27/1992 was filed by the original plaintiff Smt. Chikkapla Channabasamma W/o. Chikkapla Nagappa, for partition and separate possession of her share in suit 'A' schedule properties. The said suit was decreed on 17.07.1972, declaring that she is entitled for 1/3rd share. Thereafter, defendants have filed Miscellaneous Petition under Order IX Rule 13 CPC to set aside the said judgment in Miscellaneous Application No.74/1973 was dismissed; CRP No.2002/1974 filed against the said order was also dismissed confirming the judgment and decree passed in O.S.No.27/1992. - 11 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR 5. In the meanwhile, the original plaintiff-Chikkapla Channabasamma has sold her undivided 1/3rd share in the suit schedule properties in favour of one Chikkapla Muddappa under registered sale deed dated 03.01.1975. She had not filed petition for drawing up of final decree. Chikkapla Muddappa died on 07.11.2000 leaving behind the petitioners as his legal heirs. Thus, the petitioners stepped into the shoes of original plaintiff and filed the petition praying for drawing up of final decree. 6. After filing this final decree petition, it was duly contested by the defendants/respondents and an order appointing Court Commissioner passed on 07.10.2013 was challenged in WP No.49966/2015, which petition was dismissed as not maintainable. Thereafter, R.A.No.32/2015 was filed, which was also dismissed; against which, RSA No.271/2017 was filed, which was also dismissed, confirming the order - 12 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR appointing the Court Commissioner as per order dated 07.10.2013. 7. In the meanwhile, the Court Commissioner has submitted his report along with sketches. The said report and sketches were opposed by the respondents by filing their objections. The objections were overruled by the Trial Court by passing order dated 15.12.2021, which was under challenge in R.A.No.2/2022. The said appeal was also dismissed. Hence, the present appeal before this court. 8. While admitting the appeal, the following substantial question of law was formulated: "Whether the appellant/defendant has established that the Trial Court and the First Appellate Court have not followed Section 54 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, in letter and spirit, which leads to erroneous conclusion?" - 13 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR 9. Heard arguments of both sides and perused the records. 10. Learned Senior counsel Sri Pramod Kathavi, appearing on behalf of Sri Ramachandra A. Mali, learned counsel for the appellants would submit that; 10.1. FDP No.2/2007 is filed by the legal representatives of purchaser of suit schedule property from original plaintiff-Channabasamma in O.S.No.27/1972 for drawing up of final decree. The suit filed by original plaintiff for the relief of partition, separate possession of her 1/3rd share was decreed on 17.07.1972 granting 1/3rd share to original plaintiff. Afterwards, she has executed registered sale deed dated 01.10.1975 in favour of one Chikkapla Muddappa who died on 07.11.2000 and his legal representatives being stepped into shoes of original plaintiff have filed this FDP No.2/2007 to demarcate the share of original plaintiff and to handover possession to - 14 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR the purchasers. In said FDP No.2/2007, on 07.10.2013 the Tahashildar, Harapanahalli was directed to depute the qualified Taluk surveyor to measure and demarcate the petition schedule 'B' properties bearing survey numbers 520, 518 and 551 situated Hiremegalagere village Harapanahalli Taluk as per the extent of properties mentioned in the registered sale deed dated 01.10.1975 i.e., Ex.P.2. 10.2. The said order was under challenge before this Court in W.P.No.49966/2015 which was dismissed holding that the appeal is maintainable and not the writ. Thereafter R.A.No.32/2015 was filed which was dismissed on 17.10.2016 against which R.S.A No.71/2017 was filed and it was also dismissed. In the meanwhile, the Court Commissioner has executed the commission warrant and submitted his report with sketches. - 15 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR 10.3. The Court Commissioners' report was accepted by the Court by passing its order dated 15.12.2021 which was under challenge in R.A. No.2/2022. The said Regular Appeal was dismissed. Hence, the present appeal. 10.4. In the meanwhile writ petition was filed challenging the order passed by the FDP Court that the Court Commissioner was not permitted to cross-examine by the Trial Court. The said writ petition was allowed directing the Trial Court to record the evidence of Court Commissioner as Court witness. 10.5. The main argument of learned Senior counsel for appellant is that Section 54 of CPC is not followed by the FDP Court. 10.6. In this regard, he mainly relied on the following citations: - 16 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR 1. Sanga Reddy vs. Smt.Basamma and others1 2. K.Sham Bhat vs. K.Sitarama Bhat and others2 3. Sri.Shivanna vs. Smt.Gowramma and others3 4. Smt.Annapurna D/o. Nagappa Godi and others vs. Smt.Shantawwa W/o.Andanappa Patil and others4 5. Puttamma @ Narayanamma since dead by LRs. vs. Thimmajamma since dead by LRs 5 6. Sri G.Nagaraja vs. Sri.G.Manjunatha6 7. Smt.Obamma and others vs. Sri.Boraiah and others7 10.7. Learned Senior counsel would submit that the Court Commissioner has demarcated, partitioned and allotted shares to petitioners which is not the 1 ILR 2004 KAR 3664 2 W.P.No.6060 of 2010 disposed of on 15.11.2011 3 R.S.A.No.1434 of 2016 disposed of on 03.09.2021 4 RSA No.100266 of 2021 disposed of 30.01.2023 5 W.P.No.16179 of 2025, disposed of on 19.06.2025 6 RSA No.1223 of 2021, disposed of on 28.02.2024 7 RSA No.1595 of 2013, disposed of on 11.07.2016 - 17 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR scope of Section 54 of CPC and virtually he has not left anything to the Court. 10.8. Learned Senior counsel would further brought to the notice of this Court that, how the point was raised by the FDP Court i.e. whether the petitioner has made out sufficient grounds to accept Court Commissioner report? Raising of said point itself shows the attitude and conduct of FDP Court in not following the provision of Sec.54 Code of Civil Procedure. 10.9. Furthermore, learned Senior counsel would vehemently submitted that the order passed by the FDP Court reveals that the Court Commissioner has not only divided the properties properly, has allotted shares to appellants as well as to respondents in Sy.No.520/2, 518 and 551/E and thus the report - 18 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR of Court Commissioner does not show equity at all. 10.10.Even though several judgments were cited before FDP Court that the powers under Section 54 of CPC cannot be delegated to the Commissioner, the FDP Court has not considered all those citations, thrown out the principles noted in those citations and passed its own order. Only the extent of property mentioned in the sale deed was ordered to be demarcated by passing orders at the initial stage in FDP No.2/2007. But the Court Commissioner has demarcated the property based on the boundaries mentioned in the sale deed and thus he let nothing to decide. Without deciding which part of the property is to be allotted to the original plaintiff, she sold it and her sale in respect of only 1/3rd share can be accepted but not the boundaries mentioned in the sale deed. - 19 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR These facts were not properly considered by the FDP Court or the First Appellate Court. 11. Learned counsel for respondents Ms.Surabhi Kulkarni would submit that; 11.1. The litigation is from the year 1972. In the year 1972 the suit was filed by the original plaintiff Channabasamma against her brothers-in-laws praying for partition and it was decreed on 17.07.1972 itself and because there was nothing for her to maintain, she has sold her 1/3rd share in three of the schedule properties and not in entire 8 schedule properties. She died without issues and defendants are her Class-II heirs. They would inherit the entire remaining properties belonged to said original plaintiff but only shares are allotted in three properties which were sold by original plaintiff. After such sale, this original plaintiff died in the year 1976. Purchaser stepped into the shoes of original - 20 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR plaintiff and after the death of the purchaser; his legal representatives stepped into his shoes. Hence, the suit filed by them in FDP No.2/2007 is maintainable. The dispute went up to this Court and it attained finality. Only the appointment of Commissioner in FDP No.2/2007 was challenged and not the decree granting 1/3rd share to deceased Channabasamma is challenged. 11.2. Even though it is stated that the commissioner to demarcate the extent of property sold under the sale deed, the extent of property cannot be determined when the boundaries are there and hence it is implied that whatsoever the boundaries furnished in the sale deed, the commissioner has to measure the property within those boundaries; thus, he did not exceed his powers granted to him. The First Appellate Court has examined several aspects and the points which are raised in this appeal were not at - 21 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR all raised before the FDP Court or the First Appellate Court by the present appellants and thus, they are estopped from arguing on this point. 11.3. Para No.12 of the judgment in RSA No.71/2017 would clarify that the property which was sold by the original plaintiff is to be allotted to the legal representatives of purchaser who stepped into the shoes of the original plaintiff. Hence, considering these aspects, the Trial Court and the First Appellate Court have properly passed the order, which needs no interference. 11.4. Learned counsel would further submit that the property which was purchased by the purchaser under sale deed is to be allotted to his share as equity for purchaser. 12. Having heard the arguments of both sides and on verifying the appeal papers and the documents produced along with the appeal papers, i.e., the - 22 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR petition, orders passed by the Trial Court and the First Appellate Court, and other relevant materials produced by both sides, finding of this Court on the above substantial question of law is as follows for the following: REASONS 13. The admitted facts of the case are that, the original plaintiff has filed suit praying for partition and separate possession of her 1/3rd share in the suit schedule properties by metes and bounds. The suit was decreed and had attained finality. 14. During her life time, the original plaintiff has not filed any objection for partition or for final decree and to demarcate her share. On the other hand, due to her necessities, she has sold her 1/3rd share in three of the suit schedule properties in favour of one Chikkapla Muddappa by executing registered sale deed dated 01.10.1975. Chikkapla Muddappa died on 07.11.2000 - 23 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR leaving behind the petitioners and his legal heirs who have filed FDP No.02/2007. The FDP Court appointed the Court Commissioner by passing order dated 07.10.2013, which is extracted below: "The petition filed by the petitioners is hereby allowed. The Tahashildar Harapanahalli is directed to depute the qualified taluk surveyor to measure and demarcate the petition schedule 'B' properties bearing survey numbers 520, 518 and 551 situated Hiremegalagere village Harapanahalli Taluk as per the extent of properties mentioned in the registered sale deed dated 01.10.1975 i.e., Ex.P.2. The Commissioner fee is tentatively fixed of Rs.1,000/- Issue commission warrant if commissioner fee and PF is paid. No order as to costs." 15. In the aforesaid order, the Trial Court directed the Tahsildar/Court Commissioner to measure and - 24 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR demarcate petition schedule 'B' properties bearing Survey Nos.520, 518, and 551 as per the extent of properties mentioned in the registered sale deed dated 01.10.1975, i.e., as per Ex.P.2. The order dated 07.10.2013, reveals that the properties were to be demarcated as per the extent mentioned in the sale deed, and not as per the boundaries mentioned in the said sale deed. 16. The said order was challenged in the Writ Petition, in Regular Appeal and also finally in Regular Second Appeal No.71/2017, wherein, this Court at paragraphs 12 and 14 of the judgment, held as under: "12.The detailed narration of facts and contentions would not call for a reiteration except emphasizing the fact that Channabasavva who had succeeded in OS No.27/1972 by Judgment and Decree dated 17.07.1972 sold her right, title and interest in the joint family properties to Muddappa under a registered sale deed dated 1.10.1975. It may be that she did not institute any final - 25 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR decree proceeding and neither did her purchaser throughout his lifetime so as to take one third share purchased by him in accordance with the preliminary decree dated 17.07.1972 by metes and bounds. But, it cannot be held that respondents are strangers to the one-third share in the suit schedule properties which has been purchased after conclusion of the suit. There is also no dispute that they are not the legal heirs of the purchaser, Muddappa. Hence, it cannot be contended that they had no right to institute or initiate final decree proceeding and that they had to file a fresh suit so as to seek partition and possession of their one- third share in the suit schedule property. The plaintiff, Channabasavva did not alienate her one-third share in the suit schedule property at a point of time when her family remained joint. She had in fact filed the suit for partition and separate possession and on being successful in getting her one-third share she alienated the same to respondents' father. He stepped into the shoes of the plaintiff-decree holder and on his demise the respondents succeeded to the same. Further, the institution of the final decree proceeding - 26 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR in the year 2007 cannot be held to be hit by delay and laches. Hence, it is held that they had the right to institute the final decree proceeding. Once the final decree proceeding was filed by the legal heirs of Muddappa, the trial Court has proceeded to take further steps pursuant to the preliminary decree dated 17.07.1972 by appointing the Tahsildar, Harapanahalli to demarcate the properties which could be allocated to Channabasavva and on her demise, to her successors in interest who are the respondents herein, being the legal heirs of the purchaser of Channabasavva, namely, Muddappa. The trial Court has held that the share of Channabasavva was being allocated as per the extent of properties mentioned in the registered sale deed dated 1.10.1975 [Exhibit P2]. Therefore, the trial Court has borne in mind the fact that Channabasavva had alienated her right, title and interest in the suit schedule properties as per Exhibit P2 and equities between the parties has been adjusted by allocating the share of Channabasavva in accordance with what she had sold to respondents' father Muddappa - 27 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR under the registered sale deed dated 1.10.1975. 13. xxxxx 14. Merely because the appellants herein have attempted to assail the sale deed dated 1.10.1975 in a separate suit, the drawing up of the final decree arising from OS No.27/1972 cannot be stalled. If at all, the appellants herein were to succeed in the said suit, then at that stage, the trial Court would consider the impact of the final decree drawn in the instant case. Merely because the appellate Court refers to the respondents herein as legal representatives of Channabasavva, cannot also be found fault with. As already noted, the estate of Channabasavva was entitled to in the suit schedule properties which was crystallized by the Decree dated 17.07.1972 could have been alienated by her, and in fact, she had alienated the same to Muddappa on 1.10.1975 and the respondents herein being legal heirs and successors of Muddappa who was the purchaser from Channabasavva, have right to succeed to the said estate in terms of the decree passed in favour of Channabasavva on 17.07.1972 and on the - 28 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR basis of the sale deed dated 1.10.1975. In the circumstances, I do not find any substantial question that would arise in this appeal." 17. Thus, the order passed in FDP No.2/2007 on 07.10.2013 was upheld in Regular Second Appeal No.71/2017 and ultimately, the said order was confirmed and thus, attained finality. 18. In the meanwhile, the Commissioner was appointed who had submitted his report. The FDP Court is not allowed to cross-examine the said Commissioner. Hence, W.P.No.2242/2020 was filed, which was allowed, directing the FDP Court to record the evidence of the Court Commissioner as Court witness. 19. After examining the Court Commissioner, FDP Court has passed the order dated 15.12.2021 accepting the report of the Court Commissioner. 20. In this regard, learned counsel for the appellants relied on the following citations: - 29 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR 20.1. Judgment of the Coordinate Bench of this Court in the case of Sanga Reddy (supra). In paragraph No.5 of the said judgment , it is held as under: "5. As could be seen from the above provision, effecting partition by the Court Commissioner or revenue officer is not permissible. This is because of the change in law brought by Karnataka Act No. 36 of 1998 to Section 54 of CPC. In all cases to which the amended provision applies, the partition shall be made only by the Court and not by revenue officer or Court Commissioner. The Court, if necessary, can obtain a report from revenue officer or Court Commissioner to enable it to effect partition. It can effect partition on such a report." 20.2. Judgment of the Coordinate Bench of this Court in the case of Sri.G.Nagaraja (supra). In paragraph No.11 of the said judgment , it is held as under: "11. Further on a perusal of the mahazar and the sketch submitted by the Taluka - 30 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR Surveyor, who was appointed by the Trial Court for the purpose of division of the property in respect of item No.1, it is clear that he has excluded 7 guntas of land with structure existing thereon out of 3 acres 7 guntas and has assigned no reason whatsoever for such exclusion of 7 guntas of land with structure thereon. There is no material on record to show as to who is to conduct survey and measurement and division in respect of said 7 guntas of land in which admittedly three structures are existing. That apart the sketch submitted by the Taluka Surveyor along with the report further reveal that only the share in the property to be allotted to the plaintiff has been carved out and shares to be allotted to the other parties have been left unattended. That such a perfunctory survey, measurement and sketch cannot give quietus to the dispute between the parties. Least the same would not satisfy the requirement of law in giving effect to the preliminary decree." 20.3. Judgment of the Coordinate Bench of this Court in the case of Smt.Obamma and others (supra). - 31 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR In paragraph No.11 of the said judgment , it is held as under: "11. In view of the amendment of Section 54 of Code of Civil Procedure by Act No.36 of 1998 with effect from 1.2.2001, in all cases to which the amendment provision applies, the "partition shall be made only by the Court and not by the revenue officer or the Commissioner". The Court if necessary on the report of the revenue officer not below the rank of the Tahsildar to enable to it to effect partition, it can be effect partition on such report and court commissioner cannot make a division. In the present case, admittedly, the division is made by the Court Commissioner and not by the Court. Therefore, the substantial question of law has to be answered in negative holding that the Final Decree Proceedings accepting the division made by the Court Commissioner is contrary to the amended provisions of Section 54 of the Code of Civil Procedure." - 32 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR 20.4. Judgment of the Coordinate Bench of this Court in the case of Sri.K. Sham Bhat (supra). In paragraph No.7 of the said judgment , it is held as under: "7. The effect of the amendment Act on a pending final decree proceeding is stated in Sections 3 & 4 of the amendment Act referred to above . For a final decree proceeding to be unaffected by the amended Section 54 of the CPC, the final order effecting partition under the unamended Section 54 of the CPC should have been passed prior to commencement of the amendment Act i.e. before 1st day of February 2001. However, if any appeal or proceeding had been initiated against such final order, whether prior to or after the commencement of the amendment Act, it shall be continued or disposed of in accordance with the unamended Section 54 of the CPC as if it had continued to be in force and as if the amendment Act had not been enacted. In the present case, admittedly, no final order effecting partition under the unamended Section - 33 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR 54 of the CPC was passed prior to commencement of the amendment Act. Therefore, as per Section 3 referred to above , the papers that were transmitted to the Deputy Commissioner have to be re-transmitted to the concerned Court which shall make partition in accordance with Section 54 of the СРС amended by the amendment Act. Hence, the impugned as order of the Tribunal is in accordance with the amendment Act. I find no legal infirmity in the impugned order to warrant interference." 20.5. Judgment of the Coordinate Bench of this Court in the case of Sri.Shivanna (supra). In paragraph Nos.10 and 11 of the said judgment , it is held as under: "10. In this regard, I have carefully considered the Commissioners report produced in the paper book from page Nos.36 to 55. A perusal of page No.44 of the paper book would indicate that the Court Commissioners have not only divided the property in terms of the preliminary decree but have allotted each share to the respective parties. The said - 34 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR report of the Commissioners is contrary to Section 54 of CPC. In this regard, it is the duty of the Court Commissioners to see the feasibility of the division of property in terms of Section 54 of CPC. However, in the instant case, the Court Commissioners gone beyond the warrant of Commission and allotted the share in favour of the respective parties. However, the same has been accepted by the trial Court and same has been affirmed by the First Appellate Court. Perusal of the report of the Commissioners would indicate that same is not in terms of Section 54 of CPC, abdicates the power of the trial Court. At this juncture, it is relevant to refer paragraph 11 of the judgment of this Court in Smt.Obamma 's case (supra), which reads as under: "11. In view of the amendment of Section 54 of Code of Civil Procedure by Act No. 36 of 1998 with effect from 1.2.2001, in all cases to which the amended provision applies, the "partition shall be made only by the Court and not by the revenue officer or the Commissioner". The Court if - 35 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR necessary on the report of the revenue officer not below the rank of the Tahsildar to enable to it to effect partition, it can effect partition on such report and Court Commissioner cannot make a division. In the present case, admittedly, the division is made by the Court Commissioner and not by the Court. Therefore, the substantial question of law has to be answered in negative holding that the Final Decree Proceedings accepting the division made by the Court Commissioner is contrary to the amended provisions of Section 54 of the Code of Civil Procedure." 11. Applying the principle laid down by this Court in the aforementioned case and on re-appreciating the Commissioners report, which would indicate that the Commissioners ought not to have divide and allot the properties in favour of the respective parties in terms of the preliminary decree as it is the duty of the trial Court to 10 allot the share in favour of the respective - 36 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR parties in the suit and the said action on the part of the Commissioners amounts to abdication of the duty of the trial Court, which is not permissible under Section 54 of CPC. Accordingly, I am of the considered view that accepting the said report by the trial Court in FDP is contrary to Section 54 of CPC, in view of the law laid down by this Court in the aforementioned case." 20.6. Judgment of the Coordinate Bench of this Court in the case of Sri.Annapurna and Others (supra). In paragraph No.15 of the said judgment, it is held as under: "15. If a statute provides a particular thing to be done in a particular manner, it has to be done accordingly and not otherwise, is the settled principle of law. Section 54 of the CPC as noted herein above, casts a duty on the Court to cause partition and separate possession and allotment of shares to the parties, if necessary, upon obtaining the report from the Revenue Officer not below the rank of the Tahashildar. Though initially the learned counsel for the appellants has - 37 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR submitted that the ADLR is an officer below the rank of the Tahashildar, it is however clarified that his rank is equal to the rank of Tahashildar. In view of the law laid down by this Court in the case of RAMESH Vs. VISHNU AND OTHERS in W.P.No. 106672/2018 disposed of on 25.08.2021, wherein this Court on an identical situation, has held that the rank of ADLR is equal to that of the Tahashildar, therefore, the said contention of the appellants would not survive for consideration. However, the further reading of the provision of Section 54 of the CPC makes it abundantly clear that even though the Civil Court is authorized to appoint a Revenue Officer and obtain a report, the division of property and allotment of share has to be carried out by the Court itself and the same cannot be delegated even to the Court Commissioner appointed under Section 54 of the CPC." 20.7. Judgment of the Coordinate Bench of this Court in the case of Sri.Puttamma @ Narayanamma (supra). Wherein, the Coordinate Bench of this - 38 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR Court relying on the judgment of the Hon'ble Apex Court in Obamma Vs. Boraiah8 , it is held at paragraph Nos. 8 to 12 as under: 8. While admitting the present appeal, this Court framed the following substantial question of law: "Whether the Courts below justified in allowing the Final Decree Proceedings mainly on the basis of the division made by the Court Commissioner in view of the provisions of Section 54(amended) of the Code of Civil Procedure?" 9. I have given my anxious consideration to the arguments advanced by the Learned Counsel for the parties to the lis. It is unfortunate case, where the decree was made as long back as in the year 1981. The suit filed in the year 1975. Though the decree was passed in the year 1981 and affirmed by this Court in RSA 727/1991, till today, the decree has not been implemented. It is the mistake committed by the Court for not making 8 2016 SCC OnLine Kar 4008 - 39 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR division of the suit properties as contemplated under the amended provisions of Section 54 of the Code of Civil Procedure. 10. In view of the amendment of Section 54 of the Code of Civil Procedure, the shares or division shall be made by the Court in accordance with law, if any, on the basis of report of the revenue officer not below the rank of Tahsildar, which reads as under: "54 Partition of estate or separation of share : Where the decree is for the partition of an undivided estate assessed to the payment of revenue to the Government, or for the separate possession of a share of such an estate, the partition of the estate or the separation of the share of such an estate shall be made bv the Court in accordance with the law if any, for the time being in force relating to the particular or the separate possession of shares, and if necessary on the report of a - 40 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR revenue officer, not below the rank of Tahsildar or such other person as the court may appoint as Commissioner in that behalf". (emphasis supplied) 11. In view of the amendment of Section 54 of Code of Civil Procedure by Act No. 36 of 1998 with effect from 1.2.2001, in all cases to which the amended provision applies, the "partition shall be made only by the Court and not by the revenue officer or the Commissioner". The Court if necessary on the report of the revenue officer not below the rank of the Tahsildar to enable to it to effect partition, it can effect partition on such report and Court Commissioner cannot make a division. In the present case, admittedly, the division is made by the Court Commissioner and not by the Court. Therefore, the substantial question of law has to be answered in negative holding that the Final Decree Proceedings accepting the division made by the Court Commissioner is contrary to the amended provisions of Section 54 of the Code of Civil Procedure. - 41 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR 12. Admittedly, as could be seen from the impugned judgment and decree passed by the Lower Appellate Court, confirming the Order passed by the Final Decree Proceedings, the division was effected by the Court Commissioner as stated in the Order of the Final Decree Proceedings that, "Court Commissioner partitioned the suit properties as per the preliminary decree". 20.8. Judgment of the Coordinate Bench of this Court in the case of Sri.Malagouda (supra). In paragraph Nos.11 and 13 of the said judgment, it is held as under: "11. This Court is also cognizant of the practical challenges often encountered in partition proceedings. Though the FDP Court may appoint a Tahsildar as a Commissioner to examine and report on the feasibility of partitioning agricultural lands, in practice, Tahsildars have been known to sub-delegate their responsibilities by getting feasibility reports prepared by subordinate revenue officials. In such instances, this Court has found such sub-delegation to be - 42 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR impermissible and has accordingly set aside the feasibility reports and remitted the matters for reconsideration under Section 54 of the CPC. However, the facts in the present case are distinguishable. Here, the FDP Court has not merely endorsed a report prepared by a subordinate. Rather, it has consciously and judicially exercised the discretion conferred upon it under the Karnataka State Amendment to Section 54 of the CPC, which explicitly allows the Court to choose between appointing a Tahsildar or "such other person" to undertake the task of partition. While the first option authorizes the appointment of a revenue officer not below the rank of Tahsildar, the second option enables the Court to appoint any other suitable person, irrespective of rank, provided the appointee is competent to discharge the duties of survey and reporting. This legislative flexibility is rooted in the recognition that Tahsildars may not always be readily available, and the unavailability of suitable officers should not cause inordinate delay in the - 43 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR finalization of partition proceedings. Therefore, in the present case, the appointment of a Taluka Surveyor, a technically qualified official under the Karnataka Land Revenue Act, 1964 must be seen as a legitimate and competent exercise of judicial discretion. 13. Statutory Framework and Relevance of "Such Other Person". Under the unamended Section 54 of the CPC, the role of partitioning agricultural land is reserved for the Collector or an appointed Revenue Officer, as per the general central enactment. However, the Karnataka Amendment replaces this with a more specific and nuanced provision." 21. In the aforesaid catena of decisions, it is categorically held that the partition of properties shall be affected by the Court as per Section 54 CPC, and such power shall not be delegated to any revenue officers. In the instant case, it is to be verified whether the Trial Court has followed Section 54 CPC in its letter and spirit. - 44 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR 22. In its order, the Trial Court, at paragraph No.18, observed that the Taluka Surveyor has conducted commission work and submitted his report, thereby dividing the properties by allotting the shares to the petitioners. In paragraphs 20 and 21 also, the Trial Court has stated that the Court Commissioner has allotted shares to the petitioners as well as the respondents. In paragraph 28, it is stated that the Commissioner had divided the suit schedule properties by taking into consideration the feasibility of its subdivision between parties as per the share declared in the preliminary decree. The objections filed by respondents to the Court Commissioner's report were held to be not tenable. Paragraph 28 of the Trial Court judgment is extracted below: "28. Taking into consideration of objection filed by respondents, it is he who has to show the court commissioner has not divided properties properly, division of properties and sketch produced, if accepted, it will cause loss to the respondents. Herein the - 45 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR case as per report and sketch the R.S.No.520, 518 and 551 are divided between petitioners and respondents as block No.I and block No.II as could be seen in hand sketch. There is no reasons made out by the respondents that, division of properties as shown in commissioner report cause monitory loss to them or it is inconvenient for them. As per report and the sketch when commissioner had divided the suit schedule properties taking into consideration of feasibility of its division between parties as per share declared in preliminary decree, the objections filed by respondents to the court commissioner report are not sustainable. When court commissioner has divided properties properly, no grounds made out by respondents to reject the commission report, moreover there is order passed by Hon'ble High Court of Karnataka in WP. No.449966/2013 (GM- CPC ) filed by the respondents has stated that once the final decree court has declared that the plaintiff is entitled to the property, which is the subject matter of the sale and the same has to be measured and demarcated by the Tahasildar, - 46 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR The same does not call for interference. If after such demarcation, the defendant does not hand over possession then it has to be executed in accordance with law and therefore the impugned order passed is not in interlocutory order. It is final decree. Therefore this court of the opinion that it is just and proper to accept commissioner report and draw final decree." 23. As discussed above, in all the citations produced by both sides, it is the responsibility of the Court to pass the final decree, and such authority shall not be delegated to any other person. The Court may only take the assistance of the Commissioner while allotting the shares. However, in the instant case, the shares were not allotted by the Court; instead, the Commissioner has demarcated, allotted, and divided the property at his whims and fancies, merely on the basis of the boundaries mentioned in the sale deed. - 47 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR 24. Applying the principle laid down in the aforementioned catena of decisions and on re-appreciating the Commissioners report, which would indicate that the Commissioners ought not to have divide and allot the properties in favour of the petitioners in terms of the preliminary decree as it is the duty of the Trial Court to allot the share in favour of the respective parties in the suit and the said action on the part of the Commissioner amounts to abdication of the duty of the Trial Court, which is not permissible under Section 54 of CPC. 25. After amendment of Section 54 of CPC, partition shall be made only by the Court and not by Revenue Officer or Court Commissioner. The sketch submitted by the Court Commissioner along with the report reveal that only the share in the property to be allotted to the petitioners with boundaries mentioned in the sale deed has been carved out and shares to be allotted to the other parties have been left unattended. The - 48 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR Commissioner was only authorised to measure and demarcate the properties, but he was not authorised to allot specific shares to each of the sharers. In the instant case, in the sale deed as per Ex.P.2, not only the extent but also the boundaries of the property which were sold in favour of Muddappa are mentioned. It is to be noted here that the original plaintiff had not obtained final decree in that case, but she had obtained only preliminary decree, wherein her share was declared. However, it was not decided in that suit which specific 1/3rd portion of the properties were to be allotted to the original plaintiff; that exercise was to be carried out in the final decree proceedings. It is the duty of the Court Commissioners to see the feasibility of the division of property in terms of Section 54 of CPC. However, in the instant case, the Court Commissioners gone beyond the warrant of Commission and allotted the share in favour of the petitioners. However, the same has - 49 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR been accepted by the Trial Court and same has been affirmed by the First Appellate Court. 26. Accordingly, I am of the considered view that accepting the said report by the Trial Court in FDP and confirmed by the First Appellate Court is contrary to Section 54 of CPC, in view of the law laid down in catena of decisions. It is established that the Trial Court as well as the First Appellate Court have not followed Section 54 CPC in its letter and spirit, which leads to the erroneous conclusion. Hence, the impugned orders call for interference 27. Accordingly, the substantial question of law is answered in the Affirmative. 28. It is to be noted here that the parties have been litigating since 1972. Even though the purchaser acquired the property under the sale deed of 1975, he could neither enjoy the fruits of the purchase nor the benefit of the preliminary decree passed in favour of - 50 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR the original plaintiff. Hence, the matter requires remand to the Trial Court to obtain a proper Commissioner's report and a direction shall be issued to the Trial Court to expedite the matter. 29. Hence, this Court proceeds to pass the following: ORDER i. The appeal filed under Section 100 CPC is allowed by setting aside the judgment and decree dated 28.10.2025 passed in R.A.No.02/2022 on the file of the Senior Civil Judge and JMFC, Harapanahalli, and the judgment and final decree dated 15.12.2021 made in FDP No.2/2007 on the file of the Civil Judge and JMFC, Harapanahalli. ii. The matter is remanded to the Trial Court for fresh consideration in light of the observations made above as well as in the order passed earlier by this Court in R.S.A.No.71/2017. The Trial Court is - 51 - NC: 2026:KHC-D:6371 RSA No. 100907 of 2025 HC-KAR hereby directed to appoint Court Commissioner of the choice of both parties and to direct the Commissioner only to demarcate the properties as per the extent mentioned in Ex.P.2 and not on the basis of the boundaries mentioned therein. iii. After receipt of the report of the Court Commissioner, Trial Court shall consider objections, if any, from both sides and to pass appropriate orders. This exercise shall be completed within four months. SD/- (GEETHA K.B.) JUDGE gab CT-GTB List No.: 1 Sl No.: 31
